name: The reign of Manasseh
notes: Kings and Chronicles together recount the reign of Manasseh, the most idolatrous king of Judah, who began to reign at twelve and reigned fifty and five years in Jerusalem. Both books share the same core indictment: he did evil after the abominations of the heathen, rebuilt the high places Hezekiah had broken down, reared altars for Baal and groves, built altars in the two courts of the house of the LORD, made his offspring pass through the fire, dealt with familiar spirits and wizards, set a graven image in God's house, and seduced Judah to do worse than the heathen. Kings keeps its own vantage in the prophets' oracle of doom against Jerusalem (2Kgs 21:10-15), while Chronicles keeps its own vantage in Manasseh's captivity in Babylon, his humbling, prayer, and restoration (2Chr 33:11-16); these unique passages are left unlinked, each book's complementary view of the one reign and a matter for further study. The accounts close in agreement: Manasseh slept with his fathers, and Amon his son reigned in his stead.
